coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of white fluorescent pyrrolic nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Far'ain Md Noor, Noor
Saiful Badri, Muhammad Ashraf
Salleh, Muhamad Mat
Umar, Akrajas Ali
coredata.dc:description: Generation of bright white-light emitting diode is essential for electronic indicator and low-power lighting applications. Therefore, synthesis of white light emitting material with high quantum efficiency is highly desired. Herein, we report a facile approach to synthesize white light emitting material, i.e. pyrrolic nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots (pN-GQDs) via a solvothermal method. It was found that the pN-GQDs photoluminescence properties, i.e. emission wavelength, intensity and spectrum width, depend on the concentration of pyrolic nitrogen. In typical process, the pN-GQDs sample can emit white light, spectrum width as high as 350 nm, with brightness increase with the increasing of pyrrolic nitrogen. This characteristic is promising for white light emitting diode fabrication, hence lighting application. The synthetic approach of pN-GQDs preparation and characterization will be discussed.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Quantum dots
White light emission
Nitrogen doping

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure body mmlmath acknowledge affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Pyrrolic nitrogen doped quantum dots (pN-GQDs) synthesis

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Simple illustrated diagram of method to synthesis (pN-GQDs).
Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image of GQD prepared at different reaction times. (A) 0.5, (B) 1, (C) 2, (D) 3, (E) 5 and (F) 8 h. The insets are the size distribution of GQDs sample.
High resolution TEM (HRTEM) of GQD samples prepared at (A) 0.5, (B) 1, (C) 2, (D) 3, (E) 5 and (F) 8 h.
XPS spectrum of GQDs sample for (a) full scan survey, (b), (c) and (d) narrow scan of the C1s, N1s and O1s respectively.
(A) FTIR spectra of citric acid and GQDs and (B) Raman spectrum of GQDs.

Illustration colour of GQDs sample prepared at (A) ambient and (B) under UV light for 0.5,1,2,3,5 and 8 h. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
PL spectra of prepared GQDs sample at (A) 0.5, (B) 1, (C) 2, (D) 3, (E) 5, and (F) 8 h at excitation wavelength of 345 nm.
Deconvolution of PL spectrum of GQDs with size for (a) 4.22, (b) 5.18, (c) 5.85 and (d) 6.3 nm.

PL lifetime spectra of GQDs sample at excitation wavelength λ = 345 nn with different emission wavelength that represent blue, green and red-light. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Absorbance spectra of GQDs with difference size (a) 2.86 (b)3.98 (c)4.22 (d)5.18 (e)5.85 and (f) 6.3 nm.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of white fluorescent pyrrolic nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute Microengineering and Nanoelectronic (IMEN), Universiti Kebangsaan Malaysia, 43600 Bangi, Selangor, Malaysia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Generation of bright white-light emitting diode is essential for electronic indicator and low-power lighting applications. Therefore, synthesis of white light emitting material with high quantum efficiency is highly desired. Herein, we report a facile approach to synthesize white light emitting material, i.e. pyrrolic nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots (pN-GQDs) via a solvothermal method. It was found that the pN-GQDs photoluminescence properties, i.e. emission wavelength, intensity and spectrum width, depend on the concentration of pyrolic nitrogen. In typical process, the pN-GQDs sample can emit white light, spectrum width as high as 350 nm, with brightness increase with the increasing of pyrrolic nitrogen. This characteristic is promising for white light emitting diode fabrication, hence lighting application. The synthetic approach of pN-GQDs preparation and characterization will be discussed.

ce:section.ce:para: In few past several years, quantum dots (QDs) of II-IV compound such as CdSe and CdTe have been recognized as the leading luminescence materials due to the high quantum yield, excellent optical and electronic properties that surely bring beneficial in optoelectronic fields [1]. However, the hazardous and toxicity nature of most metal-based QDs limit the production of environmental friendly device application. Therefore, to prepare quantum dots with similar characteristic but friendly to environment should be continuously demonstrated. Recently, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have come as potential candidate for environmental friendly light emitting material due to its extraordinary optical, structural and electronic properties generated by the edge and quantum confinement effects [2]. In addition, its properties can be modified via doping with metal [3] and non-metal doped, i.e. boron [4], sulphur [5], selenium [6], chlorine [7] and nitrogen [8], producing wide variety of light emission from deep blue to near-infrared. Owing to these unique properties, GQDs materials should find extensive used in light emitting diode (LED).

There are wide range of strategy to synthesize GQDs. In generally, they can be classified into two methods: top down and bottom up method. Top-down method is breaking down a bulk material into nanoparticles including acidic oxidation [9], electrochemistry [10], hydrothermal [11] and microwave [12]. Meanwhile bottom-up is building up a small molecule into large size of materials involving stepwise solution chemistry [13] and pyrolysis and exfoliation method [14]. However, bottom up method provide wide advantages, including size homogeneity- and surface chemistry-control, enabling engineering quantum dots physico-chemistry properties. So far, most bottom up approach produce GQDs that only emit light in blue and green emission wavelength [15–17]. This characteristic certainly is unsuitable for producing white light emitting material. To generate white light emission, the material should at least emit blue, green and red emission. For that reason, it is required synthetic method to modify the photoluminescence properties GQDs in order to fulfilling such prerequisite. Doping in GQDs including metal, non-metal or organic molecule doping is well known strategy to enhance the optical properties of GQDs. Recently, organic molecule doping, i.e. pyrolic nitrogen, pyridinic, graphitic, etc., has received great deal of attention for producing white light GQDs. Here, we report a facile approach for the synthesis of multiple colour emitting GQDs of size in the range 5–10 nm by pyrolic nitrogen doping via solvothermal method using citric acid as the precursor and dimethylformamide as the medium to produce bright white light emission. By controlling the concentration of pyrolic nitrogen in the GQDs, the white light emission quality that is represented by the emission colour component of red, green and blue can be finely tuned. In the typical process, white light emission with spectrum width as broad as 350 nm can be achieved. The pN-GQDs should be extensively applicable for white light emitting diode application.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Pyrrolic nitrogen doped quantum dots (pN-GQDs) synthesis
ce:section.ce:para: GQD were synthesized through facile hydrothermal method as reported in previous literature with modifications [18–20]. In short, 0.105 g of citric acid, 0.17 g of urea and 5 ml of dimethylformamide (DMF) were added into 5 ml of bottle and keep stirring until a clear solution was formed. Then, the bottle was transferred into Teflon-lined autoclave and placed in oven for different heating time 0.5, 1, 2, 3, 5 and 8 h at 180 °C. After cooling down to room temperature, GQD was obtained by adding hexane in the solution and centrifuge at 6000 rpm for 30 min. After centrifuge, the supernatant was discarded. This step was repeated for few times until GQD in solid forms was obtained.

The surface morphology, optical properties and chemical composition of as prepared GQD are been analysed. High resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images are performed on FEI Tecnai G2 F20 operating at accelerating voltage of 200 kV and 10−6 with X-twin objectives lens. Diluted graphene quantum dot is drop drying onto carbon microgrid. Raman spectra are acquired using a WITEC Raman spectroscopy connected to a confocal microscope with laser excitation of 633 nm. The graphene quantum dots in solid form is supported on glass slide.

Chemical composition of GQD were analysed with Fourier transforming infrared (FTIR) spectra using Perkin Elmer Spectrum Instruments BX Infrared Spectrometer in range from 600 to 4000 cm−1 and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy using Kratos Axis DLD. For FTIR analysis, solid citric acid has been analyse to compare the elemental composition between the precursor and resultant GQD.

For photoluminescence characterization, the excitation and emission spectra of GQD solution were recorded using Perkin Elmer LS 55 Luminescence Spectrometer with Xe lamp as excitation source and the optical absorptions were conducted on a Perkin Elmer Lambda 900/UV/VIS/NIR Spectrophotometer with optical grade quartz cuvettes. DMF is scanned as baseline for absorption of graphene quantum dots. The optical characterization has been analysed for all variable of GQD. Prior to emission spectroscopy, we find the suitable excitation for each sample. We carried out by using excitation = 345 nm. Besides, the PL decay was carried out by FLS920 fluorescence spectroscopy (Edinburgh Instrument).

The quantum yield (QY) was determined. Quinine sulphate, Flouresin and Rhodamine B were selected as a standard to calculate the QY of the GQDs sample. This three standard were covered blue, green and red-light emission. The sample was dissolve in DMF at different concentration, while quinine sulphate dissolve in 0.1 M sulphuric acid (H2SO4), flouresin in 0.1 sodium hydroxide (NaOH) and Rhodamine B was dissolve in ethanol. All the absorbance values of the solutions at the excitation wavelength were measured with UV–Vis spectrophotometer. Photoluminescence (PL) emission of all the sample solutions were recorded by Perkin Elmer LS 55- Luminescence Spectrophotometer at excitation of 396 nm (Quinine sulphate), 496 nm (Flouresin) and 510 nm (Rhodamine B). The integrated flouresecnce intensity is the area under the PL curve at different wavelength range for every standard which is covered blue until red light emission. A graph, was then plotted using the integrated fluorescence intensity against the absorbance and the trend line was added for each curve with intercept at zero. Absolute values were calculated according to the following equation [21]:
(1)
Φ X
=
Φ
S T
(
G r a
d X
G r a
d
S T
)
(
η x 2
η
S T
2
)
Where Φ is QY, subscripts x and st represents test and standard respectively, Grad is the slope of fluorescence intendity versus absorbance plot, η is the refractive index of the solvent (
η = 1.33
for NaOH, H2SO4 and ethanol, while η = 1.4305 for DMF).

A hydrothermal technique has been widely applied for the synthesis of typical material. In this study, hydrothermal method has been used to synthesis pyrrolic nitrogen doped quantum dots (pN-GQDs) by using DMF as a solvent. Fig. 1 shows the simple illustration to described the method to synthesis that materials. In this The morphology and structure of GQDs are determined by transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM). Fig. 2 shows HRTEM images of GQD that has been prepared through pyrolysis of citric acid via solvothermal method at 180 °C with different reaction times (0.5, 1, 2, 3, 5 and 8 h). As can be seen from the figure, the average size of GQD increase with the increasing of the reaction times. In the typical process, the size of GQD prepared at 0.5, 1, 2, 3, 5 and 8 h (Fig. 2A–F), as confirmed by size distribution analysis as shown in inset in each figure are 2.86 ± 0.88, 3.98 ± 1.25,4.22 ± 1.25, 5.18 ± 1.62, 5.85 ± 1.73 and 6.3 ± 1.90 nm, respectively. The microscopic analysis results are in good agreement with previously reported by Qu et al. [19]. The yield of the product of each sample are approximately up to 63%. No by product is observed indicating the present method effectively produce quantum dots structure.

The high resolution TEM image is also carried out on the samples to verify the crystallinity and the result are shown in Fig. 3 . As the figure reveals, each GQD sample exhibit the presences of lattice fringes with spacing of 0.24 nm. This is in good agreement with lattice parameter of (1121) facet of graphite [18,22], verifying the entire samples are a graphitic structure. It is also found that the GQDs samples are single crystalline in nature. Thus the present method is successfully prepared GQD.

In order to verified the element of the GQDs we perform X-Ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) on the samples. The sample with size of 6.3 nm was used for this purpose. The result is shown in the Fig. 4 . As can be seen from white spectrum (Fig. 4a), there are three peak observed, namely the peak approximately at 284.6399.2 and 532.6 eV, which are corresponding to binding energy for C (79.40%), N (7.64%) and O (12.96%) respectively. Fig. 4b shows high resolution scan for carbon binding energy. It was found that the carbon peak is fitted by three Gaussian – Lorentzian (GL) curves, i. e at binding energy of 284.5, 285.8 and 287.6 eV. The binding energies correspond to graphitic sp
2 carbon (C-C/C =C), sp
3 carbon (C-O, C-C, C-N) and C(C=O) with percentage of 66.51, 21.45 and 12.04% respectively. These results confirm that the structure is graphene like nanostructure but with existence of oxygen and nitrogen bonding on the graphene. In order to further understand the nature of oxygen and nitrogen bonding in the graphene structure, high resolution scan on the oxygen and nitrogen binding energy were also carried out. The results are shown in Fig. 4b–c. As can be seen in the Fig. 4b, there are three deconvolution peaks of N1s at 398.3, 399.96 and 401.4 eV, which can be associated with pyridinic N, pyrrolic N and graphitic N with percentage of 3.43, 92.34 and 4.23%, respectively. In the typical process, the graphitic-N replaces one C atom in the hexagonal ring of graphene with σ bond with neighbour carbon. Meanwhile pyridinic-N is substituted into the hexagonal ring via one σ and π bonds with the neighbour carbon. For pyrrolic N, it forms two π with the adjacent carbon [23]. According to the previous study [24], it stated that present of pyridinic N has no significant change in molecular orbital because N act as one π electron with non-bonding pair. In contrast with pyrrolic-N, π electron of N gives great change in molecular orbital and the band gap. In addition, the pyrrolic-N, increase the quantum yield and cause a red shifting of photoluminescence [24]. So, it can be concluded that pyrrolic N is the main product of the present synthetic approach as the existence of red shift and the white light emission in the sample. In early study by Qu et al. [20], the pyrrolic-N GQDs is also the main product in the synthesis of GQD via dehydrolysis of carboxyl and amide group. In Fig. 4 (d) the high resolution of O1s is presented. It is shown that the binding energy of O1s can be deconvoluted into two GL curves, i.e. They can be associated with C=O and C-ON/C-OC binding energy respectively with percentage of 43.43 and 51.57%. On the basis of the experimental results, it can be worth remarked that the as-prepared sample is pyrrolic p–N GQDs. It is agreeing with recently reported result [18].

To support the composition element analysis (XPS), Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) is also carried out. The result of sample with size of 6.3 nm and citric acid are shown in Fig. 5 A. As can be seen from the figure, five peak that is attributed to pyrrolic-N GQD are observed. They are 3300–3500, 2850–3000, 1621, 1600 and 1277 cm−1 which can be correlated to N-H (primary amine) stretching, C-H stretching, NH2 scissoring, C=C for aromatic and C-N stretching respectively. When comparing the citric acid with GQDs sample, the IR spectrum is exhibit a peak shift from 1742 cm −1 and 1686 cm−1 to 1674 cm−1 and 1585 cm−1 which indicate that an amide bond was appear in the product (similar to that reported by Qu et al. [18]). No absorption peak related to graphene oxide are observed. Therefore, it can be confirmed that the sample is p-N GQDs. We then further verified the structure via Raman analysis (Fig. 5B). In good agreement with FTIR, the Raman result further support that the result is p-N GQDs. As the Fig. 5B reveals there are two main peaks appear at the spectrum namely D and G band at 1359 cm−1 and 1578 cm−1, respectively. The peak ratio of ID/IG is 1.42. It is true that the D band peak much higher than G band, indicating the disorder character is overcome the crystalline character. However, it is normal in the case of quantum dots as the existence of small portion of crystalline character in the nanostructure.

We next carried out the photoluminescence spectroscopy of the samples to examine the optical emission properties. The results are shown in Fig. 6 . The original solution colour of the GQDs sample changes from colourless to yellowish-brown with the increasing reaction times, reflecting the formation of different sizes of GQD. When each of GQD solution were exposed under UV light irradiation (wavelength of 365 nm), they emit different emission colour. For example the sample size that grown lower than 4.22 nm indicates the emission of blue colour. When the reaction time higher than QDs with size 4.22 nm, the emission colour then changes to white and become brighter and brighter with increasing of reaction time and finally produced bright white emission at reaction times of 8 h.

Fig. 7 shows the corresponding of photoluminescence spectrum of the GQDs sample. PL spectroscopy analysis was carried out by using the same excitation wavelength i.e. 345 for each sample as suggested by the excitation spectroscopy analysis. In good agreement with Fig. 2 the PL spectrum of each sample is experience red-shifting with the increasing of sizes. For examples on the QDs sample with size lower than 4.22 nm, single PL spectrum centring at wavelength of 451 nm are obtain. The full width at high maximum for the sample is approximately 91.32 nm. This is quite broad in term of quantum dots emission. This could be due to the existence of many physical process during photo excitation involving energy transfer between different state in the sp
2 orbital and other vibronic energy transfer. When the size higher than 4.22 nm, a composite of red, green and blue emission bands is started to appear, producing white light emission (see Fig. 7C–F). The PL intensity increase with the increasing of QD's size. In other to understand the ratio of wavelength emission component of the spectrum, we carried out the deconvolution analysis. The results are shown in Fig. 8 . As can be seen from Fig. 8, for example the QDs with size of 4 nm indicate a PL spectrum that is composed of red, green and blue emissions with ratio of t1.3:5.9:2.8. When the size are increases, for example to 5.2, 5.9 and 6.3 nm, the wavelength composition of PL spectrum dramatically modified to 0.9:7.9:1.1, 0.7:7.6:1.6 and 1.4:7.2:1.3 respectively. From these results, it can be remarked that the whitest emitted light is produced from the sample with size of 6.3 nm (refer to optimum whiteness standard of 3:6:1 [25,26]. The presence sample should find a potential used as white light emitter for LED and lighting applications. However, the PL analysis on the sample with higher sizes cannot be carried out due to the limited ability of our method to further increase the size. Nevertheless, we hypothesis that the increase of the size might not further improve the whiteness of the emission due to the tendency of the sample to decrease its red emission portion.

Based on photoluminescence properties, the quantum yield (QY) of GQDs (size 6.3 nm) was calculated using Quinine sulphate, Flouresin and Rhodamine B at excitation 396, 496, and 510 nm respectively as reference compound which covered blue until red light emission. The QY of GQD are 45% (Quinine sulphate), 82% (Flouresin) and 46.3% (Rhodamine B), which was comparable to previous reported GQDs ( [18,19,27,28].

We next carried out ultrafast photoluminescence spectroscopy analysis in order to understand the origin of white emission in our synthesized GQDs at excitation λ = 345 nm (Fig. 9 ). In order to obtain the characteristic of individual emission of white light component from the sample, we analyse the photoluminescence lifetime of red, green and blue component, i.e. 630, 540 and 420 nm, respectively. The sample of QDs sample with size of 6.3 nm, was used for the analysis. The results are shown in Fig. 8. By using the spectral data shown in Fig. 6 an equation of fluorescence lifetime.

(τ) =
( τ 1 b 1 )
2
+
( τ 2 b 2 )
2
τ 1 b 1 + τ 2 b 2
where, t is photoluminescence lifetime which have 2 component τ 1 and τ 2, while b1 and b2 are fractional contribution. This calculation is used to calculate the PL lifetime for every emission. It was found that the PL lifetime for the emission wavelength component for the sample is 8.0, 8.8 and 2.3 ns for red, green and blue emission wavelength. The result of the lifetime in nanosecond (ns) show the appropriateness of GQDs sample used of biological in optoelectronic and application. We compare our PL lifetime result with previous report, GQDs doped with pyrrolic-N was τ = 7.25 [29], GQDs doped with non-metal was τ = 7.6 [19] and white-GQDs was τ = 9.86 ns [30]. It shows that our result is compatible with the previous study.

In normal process the emission of GQDs comes from electron-electron and electron-phonon interaction dynamics. Therefore, we remark that the emission is mainly attributed to the electron-phonon scattering dynamics in QDs samples. Actually, the contributions from electron-electron scattering should also exist, which is normally at the origin of 5–10 fs [31]. Unfortunately, due to the limitation of apparatus, such character cannot be probed.

To further study the photophysical properties of GQD, UV–Vis absorption spectroscopy analysis was conducted. Fig. 10 shows the absorption spectra of GQD versus GQDs size. The absorption spectra show two absorption band at 270-280and 345 nm for all samples, which is typical for GQDs. They can be associated with π-π* transition of sp
2 carbon domain in sp
3 carbon matrix and n-π* transition of C=O, respectively [19]. It can also be seen from the figure that the optical absorption spectrum relatively unchanged when the size of GQDs less than 4.22 nm. However, they red shifted with the increasing of size. Such red-shifting in these main absorption bands are attributed to delocalization of π electron of C=C bond of QDs. It is also noticed the appearance a shoulder peak at 490 nm when the GQDs size increased. This is associated with the existence of n-π* transition in conjugated C=N bond. It is also recognized that this band is associated with the unique character of pyrrolic-N in GQDs [29].

According to Qu et al. [19], C=O emits blue emission at wavelength 440 nm that is related to n→π* transition of the C=O when the chromophore with functional group of C=O is excited with UV light source of wavelength of approximately 340 nm, However, C=O cannot absorb a longer wavelength of light source. Nevertheless, chromophore with functional group C=N or C=S can interact with this longer wavelength to emit green or red emission. The result of UV absorption agreed well with the PL spectra as the fact of the QDs of size less than 4.22 nm have only two absorption bands, which only can emit blue emission. It is in contrary to the GQDs sample with size higher than 4.22 nm with an additional absorption band at 490 nm, which gives white emission.

We have demonstrated a facile solvothermal method to produce a GQD with a white photoluminescence. The photoluminescence depends on the size of GQDs, where they emit blue emission when the size is less than 4.22 nm and they change to white when the size further increased. The PL in GQDs is originated from π-π* transition of sp
2 carbon domain in sp
3 carbon matrix and n-π* transition of C=O. The white light emission from higher size of GQDs is produced due to the existence new mode of electronic transition at 490 nm, generating red emission that is attributed to n-π* transition in conjugated C=N bond and electronic transition pyrrolic-N ring of GQDs. The white PL of GQD may have potential application in white organic light emitting diode device.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to acknowledge the Ministry of Higher Education Malaysia for funding this work under research grants LRGS/2015/UKM-UKM/nanomite/04/04 and FRGS/1/2016/STG02/UKM/02/02.
